title: Subtle Misogyny Detection and Mitigation: An Expert-Annotated Dataset

en.general:
This paper introduces a new dataset called Biasly, designed to detect subtle forms of misogyny in everyday language. Unlike previous datasets that focus on obvious insults, Biasly captures more nuanced and indirect expressions of sexism found in North American movie subtitles. The dataset was created with input from experts in multiple fields, including linguistics and social sciences, to ensure accuracy and depth in identifying these subtle biases.

The researchers collected and annotated movie subtitles to highlight misogynistic language that might otherwise go unnoticed. This dataset can be used for several natural language processing (NLP) tasks, such as classifying whether a text contains misogyny, measuring how severe the bias is, or even generating rewritten versions of biased text to remove the harmful language. The paper explains how the dataset was developed, analyzes the types of annotations collected, and provides baseline results using standard NLP models for detecting and mitigating misogyny.

The goal of this work is to contribute to AI that benefits society by improving the detection and removal of bias in language. By making this dataset available, the researchers hope to encourage further research in bias detection, explanation, and correction within NLP. The paper also emphasizes the importance of collaboration between different fields to ensure that the dataset reflects real-world language use and addresses the complexities of subtle sexism.
en.methodology:
The research design for this study centers on the development of the Biasly dataset, which aims to capture subtle and colloquial expressions of misogyny in North American film subtitles. The dataset was constructed through a multi-disciplinary collaboration involving experts from fields such as linguistics, gender studies, and computer science, as well as professional annotators. The annotation process involved identifying and labeling instances of misogyny, including their severity and context, to ensure nuanced and accurate representation. The dataset supports multiple NLP tasks, including classification, severity score regression, and text generation for rewriting misogynistic content.

For model and algorithm choices, the study employs common NLP techniques to establish baseline performance for misogyny detection and mitigation. Classification tasks likely utilize transformer-based models such as BERT or RoBERTa, given their effectiveness in similar text classification tasks. For severity score regression, models like DistilBERT or smaller variants of BERT may be used to balance performance and computational efficiency. Text generation for rewrites could involve sequence-to-sequence models such as T5 or BART, fine-tuned on the dataset to produce mitigated versions of misogynistic text.

Baselines for comparison include standard NLP models without specialized fine-tuning, as well as models trained on existing misogyny or toxicity datasets. These baselines help contextualize the performance of the proposed methods and highlight the unique challenges posed by subtle misogyny. Evaluation metrics likely include precision, recall, F1-score for classification tasks, mean squared error (MSE) for regression tasks, and human evaluation metrics such as fluency and adequacy for text generation tasks.

The evaluation strategy emphasizes both quantitative and qualitative analysis. Quantitative metrics assess model performance on standard benchmarks, while qualitative analysis involves expert review to validate the subtlety and context-awareness of the annotations and model outputs. Human evaluation is particularly critical for tasks like text generation, where fluency and contextual appropriateness are key. The study also includes an analysis of annotation consistency and inter-annotator agreement to ensure the reliability of the dataset.

Assumptions include the representativeness of North American film subtitles as a source of colloquial misogyny and the effectiveness of transformer-based models in capturing subtle linguistic nuances. The study does not explicitly address potential biases in the film selection process or the generalizability of the findings to other cultural contexts. Additionally, the performance of text generation models for rewrites may be limited by the quality and diversity of the training data.
en.dataset:
Language(s): English

Source: Movie subtitles from North American films

Size: Not explicitly stated in the abstract (assumed to be substantial given the scope of the project)

Modality: Text

Labels: Misogyny annotations (including severity scores and mitigation rewrites)

Metadata: Not explicitly stated in the abstract (assumed to include film titles, character dialogues, and contextual information)

Splits: Not explicitly stated in the abstract (assumed to include training, validation, and test sets)
